content_markdown:
A voltmeter of resistance $50\text{ k}\Omega$ is used to measure the electric potential difference in a circuit, as shown. The cell has an electromotive force (emf) of $5.0\text{ V}$ and negligible internal resistance.

What is the reading on the voltmeter?

diagram_metadata.description: A circuit diagram showing a 5.0V cell connected in series with two resistors of 50kΩ and 100kΩ. A voltmeter is connected in parallel across the 50kΩ resistor.

vibe_explanation: The voltmeter is not ideal; it has a resistance of $50\text{ k}\Omega$. When connected in parallel with the $50\text{ k}\Omega$ resistor, the equivalent resistance of that parallel combination is $R_p = \frac{50 \times 50}{50 + 50} = 25\text{ k}\Omega$. The circuit then acts as a potential divider with $25\text{ k}\Omega$ and $100\text{ k}\Omega$ in series. The voltmeter reading is the voltage across the parallel part: $V = 5.0 \times \frac{25}{25 + 100} = 5.0 \times \frac{25}{125} = 5.0 \times \frac{1}{5} = 1.0\text{ V}$.
